coredata.dc:title: A dual-channel fluorescent chemosensor for discriminative detection of glutathione based on functionalized carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yuanyuan
Zhou, Jin
Feng, Hui
Zheng, Jieyu
Ma, Hui-Min
Liu, Weidong
Tang, Cong
Ao, Hang
Zhao, Meizhi
Qian, Zhaosheng
coredata.dc:description: A convenient, fluorescent dual-channel chemosensor on the basis of bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (BPMA-CQDs) nanoprobe was constructed, and it can discriminatively detect glutathione from its analogues cysteine and homocysteine based on two distinctive strategies. Two distinct fluorescence responses of BPMA-CQDs probe to Cu(II) and Ag(I) were identified and further employed to achieve selective detection of Cu(II) and Ag(I) respectively. Based on the BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate, discriminative detection of GSH was achieved in terms of correlation between the amounts of GSH and fluorescence recovery. The addition of GSH into BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system induces the reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(I), which could efficiently block PET process resulting in the following fluorescence recovery. Based on the BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate, GSH assay could also be established on the basis of fluorescence response to GSH. The introduction of GSH into the preceding system triggers the competitive coordination to Ag(I) between BPMA and GSH, and silver ions are finally taken away by GSH from the probe, where the fluorescence is restored to its original weak state. Both of the detection strategies can achieve discriminative detection of GSH from Cys and Hcy. The assays showed good stability and repeatability, and covered a broad linear range of up to 13.3μM with a lowest detection limit of 42.0nM. Moreover, both of them were utilized to monitor GSH level in live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Chemosensor
Fluorometric assay
Glutathione detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of GSH detection strategies based on BPMA-CQDs probe
Synthesis and characterization of bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine-functionalized CQDs (BPMA-CQDs) probe
Assessment of detection strategy for GSH based on BPMA-CQDs probe
Real-time fluorescent assay for GSH detection based on BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate
Real-time fluorescent assay for GSH detection based on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( No. 21405142 and 21275131 ) and the 973 Program (Nos. 2015CB932001 and 2015CB856301 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs and BPMA-CQDs (A), and fluorescence spectra of BPMA-CQDs at variable pHs (B). Inset: Photographs of BPMA-CQDs (a) and CQDs (b) under UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of BPMA-CQDs as a function of copper ion level after 12min of incubation time. (B) Fluorescence spectra of BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate as a function of GSH level after 16min of incubation time. (C) Fluorescence intensity of BPMA-CQDs versus GSH ranging from 0.0 to 266.7μM. Inset: The fitting curve and regression equation between fluorescence intensity and GSH over a linear range from 0.14 to 13.3μM. (D) Selectivity test of the assay toward GSH. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of different composition including Hcy, Cys, AA, Tyr, Phe, Cystine, Trp, Glu, Orn, His, Met, Ser, Arg, Gly, Na2S and GSH. The concentration of each composition is 100.0μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of BPMA-CQDs as a function of silver ion level after 14min of incubation time. (B) Fluorescence spectra of BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate as a function of GSH level after 12min of incubation time. (C) Fluorescence intensity of BPMA-CQDs versus GSH ranging from 0.0 to 103.3μM. Inset: The fitting curve and regression equation between fluorescence intensity and GSH over a linear range from 0.18 to 23.3μM. (D) Selectivity test of the assay toward GSH. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of different composition including Hcy, Cys, AA, Tyr, Phe, Cystine, Trp, Glu, Orn, His, Met, Ser, Arg, Gly and GSH. The concentration of each composition is 100.0μM.

Confocal microscope images following addition of to HeLa cells. (A) : Only HeLa cells; (B): HeLa cells incubated with N-methylmaleimide (NMM; 1mM) for 30min; (C): HeLa cells pretreated with NMM (1mM) for 30min, and then incubated with BPMA-CQDs (0.3mg/mL) for 10h; (D): HeLa cells pretreated with NMM (1mM) for 30min and incubated with BPMA-CQDs (0.3mg/mL) for 10h and then added to Cu2+ (100μM) for 20min; (E): the system (D) +GSH (200µM, 20min). The first, second and third line images represent fluorescence, bright field and overlap field respectively. Scale bar, 20µm.

Confocal microscope images following addition of to HeLa cells. (A) : Only HeLa cells; (B): HeLa cells incubated with N-methylmaleimide (NMM; 1mM) for 30min; (C): HeLa cells pretreated with NMM (1mM) for 30min, and then incubated with BPMA-CQDs (0.15mg/mL) for 10h; (D): HeLa cells pretreated with NMM (1mM) for 30min and incubated with BPMA-CQDs (0.15mg/mL) for 10h and then added to Ag+ (30μM) for 20min; (E): the system (D) +GSH (200µM, 20min). The first, second and third line images represent fluorescence, bright field and overlap field respectively. Scale bar, 20µm.
Schematic illustration of the dual-channel detection strategy for GSH based on BPMA-CQDs probe.

head.ce:title: A dual-channel fluorescent chemosensor for discriminative detection of glutathione based on functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient, fluorescent dual-channel chemosensor on the basis of bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (BPMA-CQDs) nanoprobe was constructed, and it can discriminatively detect glutathione from its analogues cysteine and homocysteine based on two distinctive strategies. Two distinct fluorescence responses of BPMA-CQDs probe to Cu(II) and Ag(I) were identified and further employed to achieve selective detection of Cu(II) and Ag(I) respectively. Based on the BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate, discriminative detection of GSH was achieved in terms of correlation between the amounts of GSH and fluorescence recovery. The addition of GSH into BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system induces the reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(I), which could efficiently block PET process resulting in the following fluorescence recovery. Based on the BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate, GSH assay could also be established on the basis of fluorescence response to GSH. The introduction of GSH into the preceding system triggers the competitive coordination to Ag(I) between BPMA and GSH, and silver ions are finally taken away by GSH from the probe, where the fluorescence is restored to its original weak state. Both of the detection strategies can achieve discriminative detection of GSH from Cys and Hcy. The assays showed good stability and repeatability, and covered a broad linear range of up to 13.3μM with a lowest detection limit of 42.0nM. Moreover, both of them were utilized to monitor GSH level in live cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (γ-l-glutamyl-l-cysteinylglycine, GSH), a thiol-containing tripeptide, is renowned for a requisite endogenous antioxidant, and plays a vital role in defending cellular components against reactive oxygen species (ROS) and toxins (Lu, 1999). If cells are under the oxidized circumstances, it will break the balance between GSH and its oxidized form, glutathione disulfide (GSSG). Under the effect of GSH reductase, GSSG then is promptly transformed into GSH to relieve the oxidative stress of cells. Previous research indicated that intracellular GSH can be served as a crucial clinical biomarker for the reason that enormous clinical diseases are correlated with the alternation of the intracellular redox status (ratio GSSG of to GSH) (Armstrong et al., 2002). Meanwhile, an abnormally descending level of GSH is directly associated with retarded growth of children, dermatosis, hepatic impairment, and even some diseases such as Alzheimer disease, AIDS, cancer, cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, and others (Dennany et al., 2011). Therefore, the development of highly simple, rapid, sensitive and selective detection assays of GSH is in urgent demand for practical application in clinical diagnosis.

In recent decades, a host of detection approaches are utilized to monitor or quantify the variations of GSH in previous literatures, involving high performance liquid chromatography (Bayram et al., 2014; Janes et al., 2010), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Huang et al., 2009; Saha and Jana, 2013), electrochemistry (Pacsial-Ong et al., 2006; Safavi et al., 2009), electrogenerated chemiluminescence (Niu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2009), colorimetry (Hu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2011; Xianyu et al., 2015) and fluorescence spectroscopy (Huang et al., 2013; Ju et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2015). In comparison with other analytical methods which usually suffer from their own non-negligible intrinsic shortcomings such as lengthy operation process, expensive instrument and apparatus, intricate extraction systems (Yuan et al., 2013), fluorometric approach is the most widespread, convenient and promising detection approach due to its operational simplicity, inexpensive cost, high sensitivity and real time monitoring (Yang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014). Currently, the assays based on molecular probes often have some deficiencies including tedious synthesis and instability to light (Niu et al., 2012; Samarasinghe et al., 2014; Yin et al., 2014), while those based on semiconductor quantum dots have been concerned about the environmental hazard and potential toxicity (Liu et al., 2010). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new rising fluorescent nanomaterial, have shown great potential in biosensor and bioimaging (Baker and Baker, 2010; Lim et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2015). Recent advances of CQDs in analysis and sensing promise its practical application in detection of chemical/biological target of interest (Du and Guo, 2016; Jahanbakhshi and Habibi, 2016; Tang et al., 2016). Up to date, several papers have reported fluorescent detection of GSH based on CQDs. One kind of them exploited FRET strategy to detect GSH using carbon quantum dots and MnO2 nanosheet (Cai et al., 2015; He et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015), and the others utilized the disaggregation mechanism to quantify GSH (Gu et al., 2015; Hou et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2014). In general, cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy) can seriously interfere with detection of GSH among common biological species because they possess similar chemical behavior. From the previous reports, it can be seen that discriminative detection of GSH from Cys and Hcy was achieved in only two papers (Shi et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015). Therefore, it is in great demand to develop novel discriminative assays of GSH based on biocompatible CQDs.

In this study, we constructed a convenient fluorescence turn-on/off chemosensor on the basis of bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (BPMA-CQDs) which can discriminatively detect GSH from its analogues Cys and Hcy. On the basis of BPMA-CQDs probe, a dual-channel fluorescent chemosensor was constructed in two different routes, and both of them can discriminatively assay GSH with high selectivity. One route is based on BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system in terms of the facts that the coordination of Cu(II) to BPMA site of the probe leads to severe quenching of the fluorescence, and the following addition of GSH into BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system induces the fluorescence recovery of the probe. The other route is based on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate according to the facts that fluorescence enhancement of the probe takes place due to the attachment of a vast number of Ag(I) on the surface of BPMA-CQDs; the following introduction of GSH into the preceding system triggers the competitive coordination to Ag(I) between BPMA and GSH, where the fluorescence is restored to its original weak state. The two distinctive fluorescence responses to GSH of BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) and BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) are readily used to establish fluorescence turn-ON/OFF detection strategies for GSH. More importantly, both of them can achieve discriminative detection of GSH from Cys and Hcy. Intracellular detection of GSH using the assay was also conducted, and positive results were achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of GSH detection strategies based on BPMA-CQDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots are used as the fluorophore in the probe because they possess many advantages such as excellent aqueous solubility, stable fluorescence emission and abundant surface groups like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b). Bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine (BPMA) shows a strong specific recognition ability to Cu(II) and Cd(II) (Liu et al., 2015), and thus was chosen as the functional group in the probe. Scheme 1 shows two detection strategies for GSH based on BPMA-CQDs probe. BPMA-CQDs probe was prepared through EDC/NHS coupling reaction, and shows bright bluish green fluorescence in water. It is found that the coordination of Cu(II) to BPMA site of the probe results in severe quenching of the fluorescence, whereas the complexation of Ag(I) to the same position causes substantial fluorescence enhancement. The fluorescence quenching can be attributed to photoinduced electron transfer between CQDs and Cu(II), and thus the addition of GSH into BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system induces the fluorescence recovery of the probe, because Cu(II) in the probe was reduced to Cu(I) which could efficiently block PET process. In the other route, the attachment of a vast number of Ag(I) on the surface of BPMA-CQDs causes an enrichment of positive charges from Ag(I) on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate, which mainly contributes to the fluorescence enhancement of the probe. The following introduction of GSH into the preceding system triggers the competitive coordination to Ag(I) between BPMA and GSH, and silver ions are finally taken away by GSH from the probe, where the fluorescence is restored to its original weak state. The two distinctive fluorescence responses to GSH of BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) and BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) are readily used to establish fluorescence turn-ON/OFF detection methods for GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine-functionalized CQDs (BPMA-CQDs) probe
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of our previous reports, activated carbon can be oxidized chemically to synthesize CQDs (Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b). As-prepared CQDs show a relative narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 5nm, and a strong yellowish green fluorescence can be observed by naked eye under UV lamp. From XPS spectra, it can be seen that oxygen (25.5at%) and carbon (74.5at%) as two major elements can be revealed in the original CQDs, and a great deal of oxygen-containing groups including carboxyl functional on the surface of CQDs is expected. BPMA-CQDs probe was synthesized using bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine (BPMA) and CQDs through EDC/NHS coupling reaction. As shown in Fig. S1A, size distribution of BPMA-CQDs falls in the range of 2–5nm, which is in consistence with that of mere CQDs. XPS spectrum in Fig. S1B clearly revealed that BPMA-CQDs probe consists of three major elements including carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. The presence of XPS signal for nitrogen in the probe confirmed that BPMA was covalently linked to the surface of CQDs through amide moiety. Element analysis displayed that the contents of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in BPMA-CQDs are 65.9at%, 19.9at% and 14.2at%, respectively. BPMA-CQDs solution shows bright bluish green fluorescence under the UV lamp, and its fluorescence is apparently blue-shifted in comparison with that of mere CQDs. This point is consistent with their fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. 1A. BPMA-CQDs conjugate has a fluorescence emission at 414nm when excited at 332nm, while original CQDs fluoresce at optimal peak of 530nm. The fluorescence lifetime of BPMA-CQDs probe was calculated as 2.6ns according to the decay curve in Fig. S2, which is much shorter than that of CQDs (6.2ns) (Qian et al., 2015a). It is strongly supported for successful preparation of BPMA-CQDs probe from substantial differences in emission peak, fluorescence lifetime and absorption spectra (Fig. S3) between BPMA-CQDs probe and original CQDs.

Due to the protonation of nitrogen atoms of BPMA-CQDs, BPMA-CQDs probe can function as charge-transfer coupled pH sensors. According to the previous report (Liu et al., 2015), the similar pH dependent quenching characteristic induced by protonation on BPMA for BPMA-CQDs probe is expected. It is observed that alteration of pH values produced a significant influence on the fluorescence of BPMA-CQDs probe. As shown in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence of BPMA-CQDs was largely quenched when the pH was lower than 1.0 or higher than 13.0, and an apparent shape distortion of fluorescence emission was accompanied. More interestingly, a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of the probe was recorded with the increase of pH values from 3.0 to 11.0. This point is consistent with pH-dependent fluorescence behavior of molecular probe in the previous report (Liu et al., 2015). Three nitrogen atoms in BPMA enable it to function as a good electron donor, and carbon nanodots can act a good electron acceptor when electron donors are covalently functionalized (Lan et al., 2015). Thereby, it is considered that photoinduced electron transfer between BPMA and CQDs may take place. However, this PET process would be efficiently blocked as the protonation of nitrogen atoms in BPMA moiety. Therefore, this PET process between BPMA and CQDs moiety is responsible for pH-dependent fluorescence behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of detection strategy for GSH based on BPMA-CQDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous report, BPMA moiety functions as a recognition site to complex with copper and cadmium ions (Liu et al., 2015). Thus, a comprehensive investigation on the effect of metal ions on the fluorescence of the probe was carried out as shown in Fig. S4. It is found that copper ion shows strongest quenching ability to the probe under specific conditions. It is noticed that the quenching efficiency ((F0−F)/F) of copper ion is up to 5 whereas the other metal ions impact little influence on the fluorescence when the concentration of each metal ion is 333.3µM. It is reasonable to ascribe the strong quenching ability of copper cation to its stronger coordination ability to the ligand and possible PET from CQDs moiety to complexed copper ions, where a similar PET process on a molecular probe was also found (Liu et al., 2015). A slight fluorescence enhancement by Ag+ and Pb2+ is also observed under the same detection condition. However, this fluorescence enhancement was substantially amplified several times for Ag+ when the condition was changed to a lower probe concentration (0.1mg/mL) and a lower pH value (pH 5.0) as shown in Fig. S4B. This may be on account of a particularly enrichment of positive charges from Ag(I) on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate. Similar fluorescence enhancement by enrichment of silver ions was also found in N-doped carbon quantum dots (Qian et al., 2014).

It is speculated that BPMA-CQDs nanoprobe could be used to assay GSH through competitive complexation to Cu2+ or Ag+ between BPMA ligand and GSH. Thus, the feasibility of BPMA-CQDs probe was evaluated as a fluorescent chemosensor for GSH detection. From Fig. S5A, the fluorescence of BPMA-CQDs probe in HEPES buffer (pH 7.0) is almost completely quenched after the introduction of 183.3µM of Cu2+ into the preceding solution. After the addition of 103.3µM of GSH, the fluorescence recovery of BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate was apparently observed. This observation verified the speculation about quantitative determination of GSH using BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate. Moreover, this specific fluorescence response to GSH is attributed to reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(I), which is further tested as follows by using hydrogen peroxide as an oxidizing agent. An excessive amount of hydrogen peroxide (10.0mM) was added to the preceding mixture containing BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) and GSH, and then its fluorescence was recorded. The observation in Fig. S5A that the fluorescence was quenched again after the introduction of excessive H2O2 suggested that Cu(I) reduced by GSH in the last step was oxidized to Cu(II) by added H2O2. Thus, it is reasonable to assume that efficient PET is responsible for complete fluorescence quenching in BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) and the blocking of this process owing to the reduction of Cu(II) by GSH results in substantial fluorescence recovery. For BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate, the fluorescence is enhanced nearly four times by the addition of Ag+ (63.3µM) compared with its original intensity as shown in Fig. S5B. However, the introduction of the same level of GSH to Ag+ into the preceding solution leads to a great decrease in fluorescence. This fluorescence quenching could be attributed to effective removal of Ag+ from BPMA-CQDs surface. This proposed mechanism was supported by the change in zeta potential of the probe with and without Ag(I)/GSH. The zeta potential of the probe is −3.0mV, and then rises sharply to +13.1mV after the induction of silver ion. However, the zeta potential returned to negative after addition of GSH into BPMA-CQDs/Ag conjugate. Alteration in zeta potential illustrated the initial complexation with Ag(I) and final removal of Ag(I) by GSH from the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time fluorescent assay for GSH detection based on BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The condition optimization including the amounts of added Cu(II) ions, and the incubation time for fluorescence quenching and enhancement was first carried out. Fig. 2A shows gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with the increase of Cu(II) ions in amount, and the fluorescence intensity reaches the minimum when the concentration of Cu(II) ions is up to 456.7µM. Figs. S6 and S7 display the change in fluorescence intensity of the systems as lengthening incubation time for quenching and recovery experiments. A good linear fitting curve can be established between the fluorescence of the probe and the concentration of added Cu(II) ions can be established in the range of 0.24–10.0μM (Fig. S8). The linear regression equation can be expressed as y=−14.3x+759.4 where R2=0.992. The detection limit for Cu(II) ions was calculated to be 72.0nM.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this system for detection of GSH was evaluated by adding different concentrations of target into the preceding BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate. Fig. 2B indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the system could be gradually restored upon the addition of different concentrations of GSH. The fluorescence intensity of the system keeps nearly unchanged when the concentration of added GSH is over 266.7µM. A good linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration of GSH can be obtained in the range 0.14–13.3µM (Fig. 2C). The linear regression equation can be expressed as y=15.92x+95.99 where R2=0.997. The detection limit for GSH was calculated to be 42.0nM. The comparison in analytical performance between our method and the others was summarized in Table S1. One can note that the sensitivity of our method is comparable to or slightly better than those of most methods mentioned in Table S1, and also comparable to those of most fluorescent methods based on nanomaterials including carbon dots, gold nanoclusters and inorganic quantum dots which varied from 10nM to 600nM. Fig. 2D shows separate addition of each kind of species into BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system does not result in an apparent change in fluorescence intensity, whereas a great extent of fluorescence enhancement can be observed after the addition of GSH at the same level as the others. These results substantially suggest that the selectivity of our method for GSH detection performs well in the presence of these potentially interfering species and especially shows excellent discriminative detection effect of glutathione from Cys and Hcy. The discriminative identification for GSH can be attributed to effective reduction of Cu(II) by GSH with respect to Hcy and Cys, which is also supported by the change of absorption spectra using BPMA-Cu(II) complex in the presence of GSH, Hcy or Cys (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time fluorescent assay for GSH detection based on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: In the other route, the introduction of GSH into BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate causes the fluorescence to restore its original weak state because silver ions are finally taken away by GSH from the probe. Fig. 3A shows a progressive enhancement in fluorescence intensity with the increase of Ag(I) ions in amount. From Figs. S9 and S10, a relatively fast response time of 14min or 12min was chosen as the optimal incubation time. A good linear fitting curve between the fluorescence of BPMA-CQDs and the concentration of Ag(I) ions can be established in the range of 0.20–23.3μM (Fig. S11). The linear regression equation can be expressed as y=10.41x+196.4 where R2=0.999. The detection limit for Ag(I) ions was calculated to be 60.7nM.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence response of the sensing systems was recorded by introducing varied concentrations of GSH in HEPES buffer solutions (pH 5.0) at room temperature. Fig. 3B indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the system could be gradually decreased upon the addition of different concentrations of GSH. The fluorescence intensity of the system keeps nearly unchanged when the concentration of added GSH is over 103.3µM. A good linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration of GSH can be obtained in the range of 0.18–23.3µM (Fig. 3C). The linear regression equation can be expressed as y=−11.71×+902.30 where R2=0.999. The detection limit for GSH was calculated to be 54.0nM. The sensitivity of this route is slightly inferior to that based on BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate. Fig. 3D shows separate addition of each kind of species into BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) system does not result in an appreciable change in fluorescence intensity, however, a huge fluorescence enhancement can be observed after the addition of GSH at the same level, which suggests that this method possesses excellent specific response to GSH in comparison with the potentially interfering species, and especially achieves excellent discriminative detection of glutathione from Cys and Hcy. The discriminative identification for GSH can be ascribed to high coordination affinity of GSH to Ag(I) with respect to Hcy and Cys, which is also supported by the change of absorption spectra using BPMA-Ag(I) complex in the presence of GSH, Hcy or Cys (Fig. S12).

It is reported that a high level of GSH is present in cells and plays an important role in a variety of biological process. To determine the biological relevance of the CQDs-based probe, studies were carried out to assess the use of the probe in fluorescence imaging of cellular GSH. For this purpose, HeLa cells were utilized to test the feasibility of cellular GSH detection. Cytotoxicity test of BPMA-CQDs probe was first evaluated before cellular detection as shown in Fig. S13. BPMA-CQDs probe imparted almost no influence on cell viability at the concentration below 100mg/L, and the cell viability remains over 80% when the amount of the probe is up to 400mg/L, implying the excellent biocompatibility of the probe to HeLa cells.

As illustrated by the confocal fluorescence microscope images displayed in Fig. 5C, a bright green fluorescence is produced in the cytoplasm when HeLa cells are incubated with BMPA-CQDs probe for 30min. The results suggest that BMPA-CQDs probe is capable to permeate into cells. To diminish the effect induced by original GSH in cells, the HeLa cells were pretreated with the thiol-blocking reagent N-methylmaleimide (NMM) for 20min and then incubated with BMPA-CQDs for 30min. For the detection route based on BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) conjugate shown in Fig. 4, no fluorescence signal can be observed before the cells was treated with BPMA-CQDs probe (Fig. 4A and B), and then a bright green fluorescence is recorded after the introduction of the probe (Fig. 4C). As shown in Fig. 4D, after treated with copper ions, the cells show almost no fluorescence, and then the following fluorescence recovery can be viewed after the treatment of GSH (Fig. 4E), supporting BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) reacting with GSH to produce discernible fluorescence signal. For the other route based on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I), a weak fluorescence shown in Fig. 5C is originated from a low concentration of BPMA-CQDs probe, and then a substantially enhanced fluorescence is due to the complexation between the probe and Ag(I) ions (Fig. 5D). The following fluorescence quenching recorded (Fig. 5E) after the addition of GSH suggests the detection route can be run in living cells. These results demonstrated that both the two detection routes can be used to achieve GSH detection in living cells, and thus can run well inside cells in a real-time manner.

In summary, we constructed a convenient fluorescence turn-on/off dual-channel chemosensor on the basis of BPMA-CQDs nanoprobe which can discriminatively detect glutathione (GSH) from its analogues cysteine and homocysteine. On the basis of BPMA-CQDs nanoprobe, a dual-channel chemosensor was constructed through two different detection routes. One route is based on BPMA-CQDs/Cu(II) system, while the other route is based on BPMA-CQDs/Ag(I) conjugate. Both of them can achieve discriminative detection of GSH from Cys and Hcy. The assays showed good stability and repeatability, and covered a broad linear range with a low detection limit. Cellular GSH imaging experiments demonstrated that the dual-channel chemosensor can perform well inside human HeLa cells. This framework highlights the significance of further functionalization of carbon quantum dots in biological application, and shows bright prospect in practical application in medical diagnostics of GSH-associated diseases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405142 and 21275131) and the 973 Program (Nos. 2015CB932001 and 2015CB856301).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.07.081.
